summary: Gruber reveals that 'Marble,' an internal Apple UI project he had speculated about for over a year, turned out to be Xcode 4 rather than a new system-wide Mac OS X theme. He corrects his earlier speculation with information gathered at WWDC 2010, describing Xcode 4 as a major overhaul focused on single-window workflow. He also corrects a previous claim about the iPad's OS 3.2 being developed by a secret separate team, clarifying that the teams were aware of each other's work. Finally, he explains that iOS 4.0's lack of iPad support was simply a timing issue driven by the iPhone 4 launch schedule.
key_points: The secret Apple project 'Marble' was Xcode 4, not a new system-wide Mac OS X UI theme as Gruber had predicted
Xcode 4 represents a major interface overhaul, integrating Interface Builder into a single-window experience
The iPad OS 3.2 fork was not developed by a secret team — both the 3.2 and 4.0 teams knew about each other's work
iOS 4.0 shipped without iPad support purely due to timing pressure around the iPhone 4 launch
money_quotes: Ends up I was wrong about what 'Marble' is — and in fact, it was announced at WWDC 2010 this month. It's Xcode 4.
It is, to say the least, a major overhaul — primarily focused on doing more within a single window, even going so far as to bake Interface Builder into Xcode itself.
The team wasn't separate — those working on 4.0 knew what was going on with the iPad and those working on the iPad knew what was going on with 4.0.

Apple wanted to start selling the iPhone 4 this month, June. The iPhone 4 required iOS 4. And it took less time for iOS to support only iPhones and iPod Touches than it would have to add support for iPads, too.
key_insight: Gruber publicly corrects his own earlier speculation about Apple's secret 'Marble' project, demonstrating that even well-sourced Apple watchers get details wrong, while offering insider context on Apple's pragmatic engineering tradeoffs around iOS platform unification.
